SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments are solid: evaluations on standard datasets (MR, SST) with comparisons to multiple strong baselines (LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN, NCSL) and reported accuracies support the main claims. Ablation studies are provided and show the importance of NSR/SR, and targeted analyses demonstrate the effectiveness of negation/intensity regularizers on relevant subsets. Related work coverage is described as thorough and several limitations are explicitly discussed (no explicit negation scope modeling; class-level vs word-level sentiment shifts due to data size). Minor gaps remain (e.g., no code release mentioned, and word-specific shifting is not fully explored), so the work is strong but not perfectly comprehensive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new methodic element—four linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) that enforce margin-based symmetric KL constraints and word-specific transformation matrices for negation/intensity—applied to LSTM/Bi-LSTM representations. While sentence-level sentiment classification is a well-studied problem, the particular way lexical knowledge and sentiment drift/intensity are encoded as regularizers (and combined across forward/backward LSTM via a min operator) is a non-trivial and well-differentiated extension beyond standard LSTM/Tree-LSTM and lexicon-integration baselines. The contribution is likely to be informative to experts as it captures linguistic roles (negators/intensifiers) from sentence-level supervision without heavy syntactic modeling.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from problem and proposed LR-LSTM/LR-Bi-LSTM models through methodology and experiments (datasets, baselines, ablation) to conclusions; the main contributions and linguistic regularizers are stated early and repeatedly. Notation and method description appear consistent and concrete (e.g., margin-based symmetric KL divergence, transformation matrices for negation/intensity), and experiments/tables (accuracy results, ablations) effectively support the claims. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, though I infer there may be minor presentation or terminology polish remaining rather than major clarity problems.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—incorporating lexicons, negation and intensity into neural sentiment models—by proposing four linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) applied to LSTM/Bi-LSTM and demonstrating empirical gains (e.g., 82.1% on MR, 48.6% on SST). The finding that NSR/SR drive most gains while NR/IR help on negator/intensifier subsets shows a useful, empirically supported way to inject linguistic knowledge using only sentence-level annotations. However, limitations such as not modeling negation scope and relying on sequence LSTMs (plus class-level rather than word-level shifting unless more data is available) make the contribution incremental rather than transformative, though it does open reasonable avenues for further work on linguistically regularized neural models.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a solid set of relevant baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN/CNN-Tensor, DAN, and NCSL) on the same datasets (MR and SST) using the same metric (accuracy), so comparisons appear fair. The authors include ablation studies that analyze which regularizers drive improvements and relate their approach to prior lexicon-aware work (NCSL), indicating the differences are clearly discussed. However, the summary does not explicitly state whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, a minor omission in the comparative discussion.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it proposes linguistically-motivated regularizers for LSTM/Bi-LSTM that incorporate sentiment lexicons, negation, and intensity, a clear NLP modeling contribution. The paper targets the right audience (NLP/ML researchers) and uses standard problem formulation and evaluation (sentence-level sentiment on MR and SST, accuracy, comparisons to LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN, etc.). Methods and evaluation standards are appropriate (baselines, ablations, reported hyperparameters), and the work is positioned with thorough related work coverage and clear relevance to the community.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims (LR-LSTM/LR-Bi-LSTM with four linguistic regularizers) are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence on standard datasets (MR, SST) with reported accuracies (e.g. 82.1% on MR, 48.6% on SST) and ablation studies showing NSR/SR importance and effectiveness of negation/intensity regularizers on targeted subsets. The mathematical approach (margin-based symmetric KL divergence constraints, class- and word-specific transformations) is plausible and internally consistent as described. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and reported hyperparameters, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and the acknowledged omission of negation scope modeling is a limitation that introduces a mild unsupported assumption. Overall the technical claims are well-supported though with minor methodological gaps.

main_claims: Proposes linguistically regularized LSTM models (LR-LSTM and LR-Bi-LSTM) that incorporate sentiment lexicons, negation words, and intensity words for sentence-level sentiment classification.

Introduces four linguistic regularizers—Non-Sentiment Regularizer (NSR), Sentiment Regularizer (SR), Negation Regularizer (NR), and Intensity Regularizer (IR)—that use margin-based symmetric KL divergence constraints to model sentiment drift and intensity effects.

Applies the regularizers to bidirectional LSTM (using a min operator over forward/backward transformed distributions) and shows that the models can capture the linguistic roles of sentiment words, negation words, and intensity words while being trained with sentence-level annotation only.

Ablation experiments indicate NSR and SR are most important for performance, and the negation and intensity regularizers are effective (especially on subsets containing negators/intensifiers); reported accuracies for LR-Bi-LSTM include 82.1% on MR and 48.6% on SST.
_summary.methodology: Add linguistically motivated regularizers to LSTM/Bi-LSTM training: enforce small changes for non-sentiment words (NSR), apply class-specific shifting distributions for sentiment words (SR), and use word-specific transformation matrices for negation (NR) and intensity (IR); regularizers use a margin-based symmetric KL divergence loss and are combined with Bi-LSTM representations by taking the minimum of forward/backward transformed distribution distances.
limitations: The modification span (negation/intensity scope) is not modeled (negation scope resolution is beyond the scope of this work).
Negation and intensity effects depend on syntax and semantics, but the work relies on sequence LSTM encoding rather than full syntactic modeling.
Sentiment regularizer uses class-specific shifting distributions (not word-specific) unless larger datasets are available; word-specific shifting requires more data.
